transcript.transcript: Myklegard, taking place on February 8, 2006 in Boise, Idaho at the Log Cabin Literary Center on Capitol Boulevard. At the Log Cabin Literary Center on Capitol Boulevard. Would you like to tell me about some of your earliest memories of Julia Davis Park? And maybe you start out by telling us why it's important. Okay, some of my earliest memories are hearing about Julia Davis Park because my dad, Tom Davis, is a grandchild of Tom and Julia Davis. His dad, Edwin, was the fourth out of five children of Tom and Julia's. No. Yeah. There was Tom, Then Hazel, then Harry, then Edwin, and then Etta. And my Dad was born right here in Boise and lived here most of the year until he was 14, no until he was 12, 1929. And then they moved to Cascade permanently. And before that, he had actually lived in Cascade in the summers and enjoyed it because he liked the out-of-doors and the ranching. His grandfather, Tom Davis, had bought a, no great-grandfather, or grandfather, this is an uncle. Oh, okay. Yep, so it would be grandfather. Okay. Actually bought the ranch up there in the 1800s and they raised cattle up there. There was a man who took care of that ranch and now when dad, dad had been up there and got to be up there on the ranch in the summertime, and then in 29 they moved up there permanently and lived there year round. So my first memories of the park were hearing about the Park and the... Because you were raised in Cascade? Cascade and Emmet. Uh-huh. And then in Vail. What it is, is we are a farming, or a ranching family, and so you could not raise cattle very well in Cascade in the wintertime. So we would buy calves in the fall and then take them, raise them all spring, take them to Cascade and feed them all summer and then sell them from there to the beef breeders. So we move twice a year. My mother's moved twice a year for 64 years. So would you ever come to Boise and visit Julia Davis Park? Not very much. And the reason being that when my parents left, when my father left Boise, the Davis family had been very wealthy. Tom and Julia had left an extensive estate to their children, to their heirs, and for a variety of reasons, including the Depression, loss in fruit prices, loss in cattle prices. The family actually went broke, And so my grandfather and his wife and family all left in a very sad situation. But I will say that my dad collected every article, everything there was about Julia and Tom, and kept them all. And we were certainly schooled in who Julia and Tom were. Well, when did you move to Boise and start listening to Berg? Well, I lived here out of college. And of course, About what year was that? That was in 1972. And in 1973, we were married. Dave and I were married. And we actually went, we had our rehearsal dinner in the park. Where? Right in one of the pavilions, right close to the playground. And then On our wedding day we went down and took a picture, the statesman had us go down and we had a picture taken right by the ducks in the pond and that picture appeared on the front page of the bride's section in the statesman along with the picture of Tom and Julia. So that would have been when? In 1973. And what year, I mean what month? June. June. June 9th. Okay. And then did you live in Boise after your marriage? We did for a year and then we left and moved to the south for ten. And then we came back about ten years, about twenty, oh twenty some years ago and came back to Boise to live permanently. We've been here ever since. With a lot of trips to Cascade and out enjoying the other parts of life. Well, after you returned here, you then had children. And did you use the park? We did. We had three kids. And we went to the park. We took them to the zoo. We took them. Excuse me, of course, they had field trips to the Historical Museum. We would come down and just enjoy the park and the roses and the basic parts of things. Now, you're on the land that Julia Davis Park sits on is still, your family still has control of that land and certain things the park has to talk to the family members about? The family members about? Well, to an extent. The Parks Department has been more than kind to include us in the decisions that are made. The one stipulation that Tom made when he named and left the land to the city of Boise, which, by the way, he started in the 1890s offering, he and Julia, offering the land to the city of Boise. But at that time, Boise was only around 3, 000 or 4, 000 people. So they really didn't believe they could take care of the land. And also, it would flood. And so they continued to offer it. And then Julia died in 1906, 1907. 1907 and that's when Tom went to city council and he said, yes you will accept the land and you will name it after my wife. But the stipulation was that if it was used for anything besides the park, the land would revert back to the family. And I think that's one of the smartest things he did, because there has been talk of putting BSU dormitories in the park. I was approached on putting a baseball field in the park. When was that? Just lately. And so there's been a variety of ideas for the park other than it being a park. And thankfully, he put that in there because for the city, it would be a financially astute thing to do, to sell that land and use the money for whatever. But in the case that it is, that is our cultural network. That is our cultural park in Boise, and there is no other one like it. None of the other parks have the amenities that this park has. So we are naturally left being very thankful that no one has been able to use it for anything else. And because of that, I think that the other entities, the museums, the zoo, everything else, feels very comfortable being in the park because they know it's going to stay there. And that's the other reason why I feel it's so important that we now honor the park and honor the family for what they did because it's our once in a century opportunity. And how often do you meet with the parts department? When they talk to you about improvements they're going to make in the parts? Well, when they were redoing the master plan, I met with them some. But of course, since now, because we have just done a design competition in which we had a variety of architects and landscape architects and artists go together to do a design competition, I have met with them a lot because we've had a lot to do. We're putting together a fundraising program to put these entities within the park to make the upgrades and also to have a celebration. And it takes a lot of time and organization to do this. But fortunately, the Parks Department is incredible, and they have offered their services completely, no matter what we needed done, and they have certainly supported us. You know, Jim Hall could have said, we really don't foresee that you could do this, and it really isn't necessary, but he wants to see it, and also the mayor, who's right behind us, standing behind us saying, this is going to be good and we're excited about this. So I feel very blessed that this has all come to fruition. Isn't that the word I want? Right. Right. So it will be, it's going to be a very busy six months before the celebration, it's about seven months, and there will be a tremendous amount of fundraising that needs to go on for it and organization and you know just basic phone calls and visiting and emails and I think it's going to be quite overwhelming for a little while. But I think at the time we have the celebration that a lot of the fundraising will be in place, people will know. I mean, this is their one chance to renovate this park. And We have had a wonderful, wonderful committee that has come together, including Marilyn, who I am talking to today, and a variety of local people who all have an interest, and it's all a volunteer interest. No one gets paid for doing this job, And it is a gift to the city of Boise. Because, you know, as you know, if you don't strike when the, what is that? The iron is hot. The iron is hot, you don't get there. And so we are getting there. How does it feel to have a park named after your family? You know, it's really quite, it's very exciting and it's lots of fun. And it's very fun to drive through the park and think, this was really where my great-grandparents were. They lived there or around there, just down the street. But I also feel that it gives, I also feel a responsibility to honor the park and to keep it moving forward. Only the family will do that. If this is left up to local citizens and the Parks Department, there are just too many other things going on. And it takes a person with a vested interest to ever make something like this happen. Because no one else has the wherewithal and the heart feeling to get it done. Are there any stories that you remember your father telling you about the family? Julia Davis? Well, yes. Julia was. Tom was. Well, actually, they were both quite the entrepreneurs because Julia was right beside Tom the whole time while he was building quite a estate. And she was right there but she also must have been an extremely kind person. Must have had a very kind heart. And the story goes that she was the welcome wagon of Boise. She would go down and help the women who were coming into town on the wagon train, help them find their way, make them feel welcome, and help them get settled. And for a woman who had quite, was quite a woman of means, she could have been traveling to wherever and doing whatever. But she chose to make Boise a better place. And the other part that I find is that they were so conscientious about making sure that Boise had a park. I mean, many people, when they had been turned down, or no one ever really honored their gift, they would have given up. But they didn't. They continued on. And I think that that's very admirable. Where did they learn about Parks? Had they lived in a community somewhere else before they came to Boise? Well, she was from Chicago, or from Canada, Galt. And he was from Illinois. But I think a lot of it came, they had traveled, they were travelers, their kids were extremely well educated, their sons in particular. My grandfather even went to Notre Dame and Exeter for high school. And so they were very conscious of how good life could be. Because of what they did, they must, it must not have just been for the dollar. There was much more to their life than that. They talk about that Julia took her oldest son, Tom, to the Chicago World's Fair when he graduated from college. Or from high school. And my grand, uh-huh, Chicago World's Fair, yeah. And my grandfather traveled. You know, It's wonderful to see a family who offers their family opportunities, does not just saddle them with responsibility. And I believe that they did a lot of that. But what are some of your hopes for the renovation of the park, some of the things that you're particularly interested in seeing built? Because They're not there now, and when people listen to this, they'll be many years later. I think one of the exciting things is that we're talking about it being a walking history of Boise and of Idaho. And I think it will give it a different history feeling than walking into a museum. Because as you walk through, you're actually on sacred grounds. You're on the ground where it happened. You're not just looking at it. And if I am, I am hoping that we represent all the parts of Tom's life, Tom and Julia. He took the first water, water right out of the Boise River. So I'm hoping that we will represent the natural resources that Boise has, whether it be water or, you know, gas, trees, all the natural. And then also he was a farmer. I mean, that's where he started. He fed Idaho City and Silver City while they were gold mining, or while the miners were there. And So he was very aware of feeding the people and continued to do that. And I hope one of the pavilions will be the local crops, whether it be potatoes or onions or whatever, fruit trees. He also brought the first purebreds to Idaho, purebred cattle. And we have quite a history of cattle and sheep here in Idaho with all the Basque sheep herders. And they were very good to my dad in Cascade. And they, they're very important. Also, just commerce in general. The grocery stores, the banks. So, when you really look at his life growing up, I mean, Tom's life is everyone's life. What happened here in Boise didn't just happen to Tom and Julia. This was really the growth of the city. And Tom helped lay out the plaque of the city. He helped Boise become what it is today. And it's exciting that now people will be able to go to the park and actually see it right where it happens. Have you had any, you said that there, were you approached about putting dormitories? It was just no let me put it this way it was no I was not but Actually was in a real estate class that someone brought it up and said, you know, the Davises. And I'm standing there going, oh, man, would this be nice? Have this land. But I can't imagine it being anything but a park anyway. And as Boise grows and as we're watching it grow, these things have to be so valued, because we only have this one. And the other part is that it started a trend. I mean, Albertson Park and Morrison Park, now we have Marian Williams Park and Simplot and I mean it just started a trend that has left the city of Boise. I mean to those who might be listening to this 30 years from now, my question is, can you imagine if Julia Davis Park was part of the BSU campus? And that Ann Morrison Park was a housing development and that Katherine Albertson was an Albertson store. I mean that could have all been. This could have all been housing right here through town and businesses. And instead The city of Boise has been honored with a whole collection of parks. And the people of Boise, there's, I remember Bill Drake telling me as he drove into town, they lived in LA, and this was in the 50s, 60s, somewhere in there. And he said they picked up hamburgers at Big Bun and came down and sat in the park by the river. And he said he didn't know that rivers ran without seam in sight, because that's the way he assumed them to run. Uh-huh. Yeah. OK. Because that's the way he was seen in the middle. Uh-huh. Yeah. Yeah. So it was. OK. Do your children, was there anything special that your children did at the park? Other than be there, no. I don't have a lot of stories that way. Not a lot of stories from you. Mine come more from Ann Morrison, because my daughter plays soccer. We spend a lot of time there. But of course, we always came down to the festies, you know, the festivities and enjoyed. Yeah, too. Was there anything that you think that people should know who might be listening to this about how important it is for, certainly if you had not stayed active, you were keeping, are you passing the torch on to one of your children? Well, I've offered it, but no one seems to be picking it up yet, but who knows? Who knows? They may. I don't mean now, I mean when you're older. Yeah, and I'm hoping that they will. I'm hoping that they will see it as a part of their legacy. Uh-huh. But, You know, they don't know the value of it yet. There will come a time when they will. When they have children, they will know the value. Right now they can win the green belt anywhere. Right. But when they have children, and they are bringing them to the zoo and the museums, and finding their world, it will become far more important. And I think, plus, you also begin to honor more the legacy of your life. Uh-huh. And I'm hoping that they will. Was Julia Davis a woman in that, all those many years ago? It sounds like she was a pretty outstanding person. That many years ago, many women were very much just working at home. It sounds like she did that much more. She was very involved. She was in many organizations. She was never an officer in any of them. But she was certainly involved. And I think sometimes it's those people in the background, like Marilyn, taking my words of wisdom that make the difference. It's easy to stand up in front of people and do that because you've got all the glory, but when you're the guts of the situation, It's harder. It's more, you're the stability that makes it go. All the people in the world can stand up and lead, but if there's no one there that's willing to work with him, they're out of luck. Well, is there anything else you can think of that you'd like to say that I haven't asked? If someone were listening to this, I would hope that they were still enjoying the park, that it is still honored and renovated as need be, and that The volunteers that keep it together are still intact. Like the rose garden. I mean, the rose garden is kept up by the volunteers. They go in and cut the roses every week. And when you're talking, we're going to have a, they're going to show all the trees, a walking tour of the trees. I just hope that when someone's listening to this that they can look back and say, you know, they really did do all this. And we're thankful that they did. Well, thank you. Thank you. Thank you for your hours and hours of work on this. Mmm. It is hours. It is hours. It is hours. Little did we know, but it's worthwhile ours, so that's OK. OK, thank you. This has been an interview with Myklegard, who is the great-granddaughter of Tom and Julia Davis.

lines.line: # Family Connections to Julia Davis Park

> The speaker begins by discussing their earliest memories of Julia Davis Park, which are primarily through stories heard from their father. The speaker's father was a descendant of Tom and Julia Davis, the namesakes of the park. The family's history is deeply intertwined with the park, as their ancestors were significant figures in the local community and had substantial influence over the development of the area.

metadata.title: Family Connections to Julia Davis Park
metadata.synopsis: The speaker begins by discussing their earliest memories of Julia Davis Park, which are primarily through stories heard from their father. The speaker's father was a descendant of Tom and Julia Davis, the namesakes of the park. The family's history is deeply intertwined with the park, as their ancestors were significant figures in the local community and had substantial influence over the development of the area.

lines.line: > The speaker explains the logistical challenges of ranching in Cascade during the winter, which shaped their family's lifestyle of moving cattle seasonally for better grazing and market conditions. This nomadic lifestyle influenced their infrequent visits to Boise and Julia Davis Park, highlighting a connection between their livelihood and their interactions with the park.

metadata.title: Ranching Background and Lifestyle
metadata.synopsis: The speaker explains the logistical challenges of ranching in Cascade during the winter, which shaped their family's lifestyle of moving cattle seasonally for better grazing and market conditions. This nomadic lifestyle influenced their infrequent visits to Boise and Julia Davis Park, highlighting a connection between their livelihood and their interactions with the park.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses the financial hardships their family faced after leaving Boise, which stemmed from the Great Depression and fluctuations in agricultural prices. Despite these challenges, the speaker's father preserved a wealth of historical documents about Tom and Julia Davis, ensuring that the family legacy remained a significant part of their identity.

metadata.title: Family's Financial Struggles and Legacy
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the financial hardships their family faced after leaving Boise, which stemmed from the Great Depression and fluctuations in agricultural prices. Despite these challenges, the speaker's father preserved a wealth of historical documents about Tom and Julia Davis, ensuring that the family legacy remained a significant part of their identity.

lines.line: # Personal Experiences at Julia Davis Park

> The speaker recounts their personal experiences at Julia Davis Park, including significant life events such as their wedding rehearsal dinner and wedding day photos taken at the park. These personal anecdotes highlight the park's role as a backdrop for important milestones in the speaker's life.

metadata.title: Personal Experiences at Julia Davis Park
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts their personal experiences at Julia Davis Park, including significant life events such as their wedding rehearsal dinner and wedding day photos taken at the park. These personal anecdotes highlight the park's role as a backdrop for important milestones in the speaker's life.

lines.line: > The speaker discusses the family's continued influence and control over Julia Davis Park, including the stipulation that the park must remain a park or the land would revert to the family. This control has helped prevent the commercialization of the park, preserving its intended use as a public space.

metadata.title: Family's Influence Over the Park
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the family's continued influence and control over Julia Davis Park, including the stipulation that the park must remain a park or the land would revert to the family. This control has helped prevent the commercialization of the park, preserving its intended use as a public space.

lines.line: # Future Plans and Renovations for the Park

> The speaker outlines the involvement in planning and fundraising for future renovations and improvements in Julia Davis Park. These efforts are part of a broader initiative to enhance the park's facilities and ensure it remains a vibrant part of Boise's cultural landscape.

metadata.title: Future Plans and Renovations for the Park
metadata.synopsis: The speaker outlines the involvement in planning and fundraising for future renovations and improvements in Julia Davis Park. These efforts are part of a broader initiative to enhance the park's facilities and ensure it remains a vibrant part of Boise's cultural landscape.

lines.line: > The speaker reflects on the significance of having a park named after their family, expressing a sense of responsibility to maintain and honor the park. They discuss the importance of the family's role in ensuring the park's continued relevance and upkeep.

metadata.title: Reflections on the Park's Legacy
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the significance of having a park named after their family, expressing a sense of responsibility to maintain and honor the park. They discuss the importance of the family's role in ensuring the park's continued relevance and upkeep.

lines.line: > The speaker expresses hopes that future generations will continue to enjoy and value Julia Davis Park. They emphasize the importance of maintaining the park's facilities and the role of volunteers in keeping the park vibrant, hoping that the park remains a cherished part of Boise's community.

metadata.title: Hopes for the Park's Future
metadata.synopsis: The speaker expresses hopes that future generations will continue to enjoy and value Julia Davis Park. They emphasize the importance of maintaining the park's facilities and the role of volunteers in keeping the park vibrant, hoping that the park remains a cherished part of Boise's community.

story.description: Recorded on February 8, 2006. Topic is Julia Davis Park.
